edges.description: Loss of Notch-driven clock oscillation corrupts periodic somite formation.
intermediate_mechanisms: loss of cyclic Notch target-gene oscillation in the presomitic mesoderm

edges.description: Mis-specified somites give rise to malsegmented vertebrae and ribs.

nodes.description: Loss of clock oscillation and wavefront positioning corrupts the periodic formation of somite boundaries, so somites form with abnormal timing, number, or rostrocaudal polarity. The metameric template from which the vertebrae and ribs derive is therefore mis-specified.

evidence.snippet: The spondylocostal dysostoses (SCDs) are a heterogeneous group of vertebral malsegmentation disorders that arise during embryonic development by a disruption of somitogenesis.
The FGF pathway establishes a posterior-to-anterior signaling gradient in the presomitic mesoderm (PSM), which controls cell maturation and is involved in the positioning of segmental boundaries.

nodes.description: Biallelic loss-of-function variants in Notch-pathway and segmentation-clock genes (DLL3, MESP2, LFNG, HES7, TBX6; also RIPPLY2) disrupt the oscillatory Notch signaling that drives the segmentation clock in the presomitic mesoderm. Proper regulation of Notch signaling is an absolute requirement for correct patterning of the axial skeleton.

evidence.snippet: These genes are important components of the Notch signaling pathway, which has multiple roles in development and disease.
Identification of biallelic pathogenic variants in DLL3, HES7, LFNG, MESP2, RIPPLY2, or TBX6 can confirm the diagnosis of autosomal recessive SCDO.

nodes.description: Because the vertebrae and ribs are serially homologous somite derivatives, the mis-segmentation manifests as a coordinated multi-segment malformation bundle: multiple vertebral segmentation defects (hemivertebrae, fused/block vertebrae) together with rib abnormalities, producing a short trunk and, in severe disease, a small thorax with respiratory compromise.

evidence.snippet: multiple segmentation defects of the vertebrae in combination with abnormalities of the ribs
Patients with spondylocostal dysostosis (SCD) have congenital spine and rib deformities associated with frequently severe thoracic insufficiency and respiratory compromise.
